name: API lifecycle & security
description: API lifecycle and security from the Dennemeyer 100: contracts and data exchange, versioning/compatibility/deprecation, OAuth/OIDC/JWT, RBAC vs ABAC, API security essentials, and integration styles (webhooks, pagination, gRPC, sync vs async).

topics.name: API versioning & compatibility
topics.description: Evolving APIs without breaking consumers: additive changes, versioning strategies, deprecation.
topics.studyNotes:
Changing an API without breaking existing clients. The version can live in the URL (/v1/renewals → /v2/renewals), a header, or the media type. The compatibility rules matter more than the mechanism: ADDING an optional field is generally safe; deleting or renaming an existing field is a breaking change — clients that expect status will break when it becomes state.

Backward compatibility in practice: support both forms for a transition period rather than switching abruptly. If you rename a field, ship both, backfill, and retire the old one only after a communicated deprecation.

Deprecation is a process, not a flag: the old endpoint or field still works but is documented as deprecated, with a migration guide and a deadline. For external customers this is non-negotiable — an integration that silently dies is the worst possible API experience. A six-month sunset with a documented replacement is the pattern to describe.

topics.name: OAuth2 / OIDC / JWT
topics.description: Delegated authorization, identity on top of it, and the token format tying them together.
topics.studyNotes:
The one-line separation that interviewers listen for: OAuth is authorization — what may this app or user do (scopes); OIDC is an authentication layer on top of OAuth — who IS the user; JWT is a token format carrying claims (userId, roles, scopes, expiry) — often the envelope for both.

In flow terms: an OIDC login tells you who the user is; OAuth scopes decide whether they may call the renewals API; the JWT carries those claims to every request, verified by signature.

They are frequently confused because they travel together — but one is a protocol for delegated access, one an identity layer, and one a data format. Any of the three can appear without the others: OAuth access tokens may be opaque strings rather than JWTs, and JWTs are used far outside OAuth.

topics.name: RBAC vs ABAC
topics.description: Role-based vs attribute-based access control models and when each fits.
topics.studyNotes:
First the parent distinction: authentication is "who are you?", authorization is "what may you do?" — login establishes identity, but every data access still needs a permission check (does THIS user see THIS customer's portfolio?).

RBAC — role-based access control: permissions attach to roles (Admin, Support Agent, Finance User, Read-only), users get roles. Simple, explainable, auditable — but rigid when exceptions multiply.

ABAC — attribute-based access control: decisions computed from attributes of user, resource and context — user.companyId == document.companyId AND document.sensitivity != restricted. Far more flexible, notably harder to test and debug.

Complete the answer with least privilege: every user, service and process gets the MINIMUM permission it needs. A reporting service that only reads should not be able to delete; service-to-service calls use scoped tokens. Start with RBAC for clarity; add attribute conditions where real rules demand them.

topics.name: API Contracts & Data Exchange
topics.description: The API as a contract; DTOs, canonical data models, mapping, and validation vs business rules.
topics.studyNotes:
An API is not a list of endpoints — it is a CONTRACT between clients and the backend: stable request/response shapes, validation, consistent error codes, pagination, versioning, auth, and documentation. External customers build on the contract, not on your code; OpenAPI/Swagger makes it machine- and human-readable so a partner integrates from the spec (request body, response examples, error codes) without seeing internals.

The layers that keep the contract stable:
- DTOs: the objects that cross the API are NOT your database entities. A RenewalRequestDto exposes customerId and trademarkId; the entity's internal notes and audit columns stay inside. This prevents leaking the internal model and keeps the contract stable while internals evolve.
- Domain model vs database model: the domain object (a Renewal with status-transition rules) may map to several tables (renewals, payments, status_history). Different concerns, different shapes.
- Canonical data model: in a data-exchange platform, every partner's format converts to ONE internal model. Two partners send different field names; internally everything is TrademarkRenewal { trademarkId, dueDate, customerId }. The core system never learns each partner's quirks.
- Data mapping: the conversion layer — date formats, enums, country codes (DE vs "Germany" → ISO), currencies, optional/required mismatches. Keep it explicit, tested, versioned.

Validation vs business rule — different failures, different handling: validation is technical form (date parses, enum known, required present → 400); a business rule is domain truth (trademark not renewable because it expired → 409/422 with a domain error). Schema evolution ties it together: consumers must tolerate added fields; removing or renaming is the breaking change.

topics.name: API Security Essentials
topics.description: Beyond login: input validation, secrets, encryption in transit/at rest, audit logging.
topics.studyNotes:
API security is not just login. The full list: authentication, authorization, input validation, rate limiting, audit logging, secrets management, least privilege, and safe error responses.

The rule that anchors everything: the backend validates EVERYTHING, because the frontend can be bypassed — the API is directly callable. Input validation covers required fields, types, lengths, enums, dates — and authorization-by-data: the user asked for customer 456's portfolio, but their token says customer 123 → deny, regardless of what the request claims.

Secrets — DB passwords, API keys, JWT signing secrets, OAuth client secrets, private keys — never live in Git or logs. They belong in a secrets manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Kubernetes Secrets) and are injected at runtime.

Encryption comes in two independent layers: in transit (TLS/HTTPS everywhere — browser to API AND service to service — protecting tokens and data from sniffing) and at rest (database, disks, backups, object storage encrypted — storage-level access does not yield plaintext). Neither replaces authorization; they are additional layers.

Audit logging is distinct from debug logging: it proves WHO did WHAT — who changed the trademark status, when, from what old value to what new value, under which requestId. For IP, payment and legal data this is a compliance requirement, not a nice-to-have.

topics.name: Webhooks, Polling & Communication Styles
topics.description: Webhook vs polling, cursor pagination, REST vs RPC/gRPC, sync vs async, schema evolution.
topics.studyNotes:
How systems talk to each other — the choices and their trade-offs.

Webhook vs polling: polling has the client ask periodically (simple, wasteful — a partner hitting the status endpoint every minute); a webhook has YOUR system call the customer's URL on events (efficient, but now you own retries, signature validation, idempotency, timeouts, and delivery logging). Webhooks are a distributed-systems commitment, not just a POST.

Pagination: offset is simple but unstable on changing lists — items shift between pages, causing duplicates or gaps at offset=5000. Cursor-based pagination (?cursor=abc&limit=50) is stable on large, continuously changing data — the usual choice for external APIs.

REST vs RPC: REST is resource-centric (POST /renewals), RPC operation-centric (POST /submitRenewal). Neither always wins: external documented integrations lean REST (OpenAPI tooling); internal high-performance service calls lean gRPC — typed, binary Protocol Buffers, fast, code-generated contracts — at the cost of being less convenient for external clients and harder to debug than JSON.

Sync vs async: synchronous calls are simple but a slow dependency blocks the caller. Async — validate the request, enqueue the heavy work (document generation), answer 202/PENDING — decouples from slow or unreliable downstreams. The UI shows pending honestly.

Schema evolution spans all of it: consumers must tolerate new optional fields (statusReason appears — old consumers keep working); deleting, renaming, retyping, or surprising enum values are the breaking changes. In a data-exchange platform this discipline is existential.

questions.title: Evolving an API without breaking clients
questions.prompt: Your public API needs a breaking change, but hundreds of clients depend on it. How do you handle API evolution?
questions.answerStructureHint: Prefer additive/backward-compatible changes; when truly breaking: version, run both, deprecation policy with communication and migration window.

expectedPoints.label: Prefers backward-compatible changes
expectedPoints.description: Add fields/endpoints instead of changing or removing; tolerant readers ignore unknown fields.

expectedPoints.label: Versions and runs both
expectedPoints.description: Introduces v2 (URL/header), keeps v1 running in parallel during migration.

expectedPoints.label: Deprecation policy with communication
expectedPoints.description: Announce, document, set a sunset date, monitor v1 usage before turning it off.

followUps.prompt: How does the same problem look for event schemas on a message queue?

questions.title: OAuth2, OIDC and JWT
questions.prompt: Explain the relationship between OAuth2, OpenID Connect and JWT. What does each one actually do?
questions.answerStructureHint: OAuth2 = delegated authorization (access tokens), OIDC = identity layer on top (ID token), JWT = self-contained signed token format.

expectedPoints.label: OAuth2 = delegated authorization
expectedPoints.description: Lets an app access resources on a user's behalf via access tokens — authorization, not login.

expectedPoints.label: OIDC adds identity
expectedPoints.description: OpenID Connect layers login/identity on OAuth2 via the ID token and userinfo.

expectedPoints.label: JWT = signed, self-contained token
expectedPoints.description: Signed claims readable without a DB lookup; validate signature and expiry; revocation is the hard part.

followUps.prompt: Where would you store tokens in a browser app, and what are the risks of each option?

questions.title: RBAC vs ABAC
questions.prompt: Compare role-based and attribute-based access control. When does RBAC stop being enough?
questions.answerStructureHint: RBAC: permissions via roles — simple, auditable. ABAC: policies over attributes (user, resource, context) — fine-grained. Role explosion is the tipping point.

expectedPoints.label: RBAC basics and strengths
expectedPoints.description: Users get roles, roles carry permissions; simple to reason about and audit.

expectedPoints.label: ABAC: policies over attributes
expectedPoints.description: Decisions from attributes of user, resource and context (owner, department, time, tenant).

expectedPoints.label: Knows the tipping point
expectedPoints.description: Role explosion — combinatorial roles per department/region/tenant — signals the need for ABAC; ABAC costs complexity and testability.

followUps.prompt: How does the principle of least privilege apply to whichever model you pick?

questions.title: Designing an API as a contract
questions.prompt: You are exposing an API to external customers who integrate against it. What makes it a good contract, and how do you keep your internal model from leaking into it?

expectedPoints.label: Contract thinking
expectedPoints.description: Stable shapes, consistent errors, pagination, versioning, docs — clients build on the contract.

expectedPoints.label: DTO ≠ entity
expectedPoints.description: API objects are separate from DB/domain models; internals stay private and evolvable.

expectedPoints.label: Canonical model + explicit mapping
expectedPoints.description: Partner formats normalize to one internal model; mapping is tested and versioned.

expectedPoints.label: Validation vs business rule
expectedPoints.description: Technical form errors (400) vs domain refusals (409/422) — separated handling.

questions.title: Securing an API beyond login
questions.prompt: Authentication is done. What else belongs to API security before you expose the service?

expectedPoints.label: Backend validates everything
expectedPoints.description: Frontend is bypassable; types/enums/lengths AND data-level authorization checks.

expectedPoints.label: Secrets out of Git and logs
expectedPoints.description: Secret manager / K8s Secrets; injected at runtime.

expectedPoints.label: Encryption in transit and at rest
expectedPoints.description: TLS everywhere including service-to-service; encrypted storage/backups; neither replaces authz.

expectedPoints.label: Audit logging
expectedPoints.description: Who did what, when, old/new value — distinct from debug logs.

questions.title: Webhook or polling — and what comes with it
questions.prompt: A partner wants real-time updates about renewal status changes. Compare polling and webhooks, and describe what a production webhook implementation needs.

expectedPoints.label: Polling vs webhook trade-off
expectedPoints.description: Polling: simple, wasteful. Webhook: efficient push, but retry/security/tracking become your problem.

expectedPoints.label: Production webhook checklist
expectedPoints.description: Retries with backoff, signature validation, idempotency, timeout, delivery log.

expectedPoints.label: Cursor pagination for changing lists
expectedPoints.description: Offset duplicates/skips on moving data; cursor is stable for external APIs.

expectedPoints.label: Async for slow work
expectedPoints.description: Validate, enqueue, return pending — do not block the request on document generation.
